schools.summary: Gill: Moses kept the flock of Jethro his father-in-law, the priest of Midian, who was either Reuel or a son of his; he led the flock to the backside of the desert and came to Horeb; the witnesses note Moses keeping the flock of Jethro and leading it to Horeb, the mountain of God — the lowly shepherd-life of Moses, the setting of the great call (Moses, once a prince now a humble shepherd, found at his daily work: 'Moses kept the flock of Jethro his father in law... and came to the mountain of God, even to Horeb'; the lowliness of his estate, that Moses, who had been raised in Pharaoh's court, now 'kept the flock' of his father-in-law, a plain shepherd in the wilderness, forty years in this hidden, humble life; the leading of the flock 'to the backside of the desert,' far into the wilderness, and his coming 'to the mountain of God, even to Horeb,' called so by anticipation, for there God would after give the law; the providence, that Moses, all unknowing, was led to the very place of his calling, God meeting him in the path of his common duty; that God often calls his servants while they are faithfully busy at their lowly work).

themes: Now Moses kept the flock of Jethro his father in law, the priest of Midian
and came to the mountain of God, even to Horeb
Moses the lowly shepherd, once a prince, led to Horeb in his daily duty
God meeting him at his common work, the setting of the great call

schools.summary: Gill: the Angel of the LORD was not a created angel but the Angel of God's presence and covenant, the eternal Word and Son of God, since he is afterwards expressly called Jehovah; the witnesses note the Angel of the LORD appearing to Moses in a flame of fire out of the bush, which burned yet was not consumed — the wondrous theophany, the divine Angel in the unconsumed bush (God appearing in fire to call his servant: 'the angel of the LORD appeared unto him in a flame of fire out of the midst of a bush... and the bush was not consumed'; who the 'Angel of the LORD' was, not a created angel but, as Gill marks, 'the Angel of God's presence and covenant, the eternal Word and Son of God,' for he is afterward called 'Jehovah' and 'God,' a pre-incarnate appearing of Christ; the manner, 'in a flame of fire out of the midst of a bush,' God appearing in fire, the emblem of his holiness and majesty; the great wonder, 'the bush burned with fire, and the bush was not consumed,' that the lowly thorn-bush blazed yet was not burnt up; the rich figure the commentators draw, of Israel and the church in the fire of affliction in Egypt, burning yet not consumed because God was in the midst; that what cannot be consumed when God is present is a comfort to his suffering people).

debates.question: Who is the 'Angel of the LORD' in the burning bush?

sides.position: Not a created angel, but the Angel of God's presence and covenant, the eternal Word and Son of God — a pre-incarnate appearing of Christ, since he is afterward expressly called Jehovah and God (vv.4, 6)

debates.question: What does the unconsumed burning bush figure?

sides.position: Israel (and the church) in the fire of affliction in Egypt — burning yet not consumed, because God was in the midst; a comfort to God's suffering people

themes: the angel of the LORD appeared unto him in a flame of fire out of the midst of a bush
and, behold, the bush burned with fire, and the bush was not consumed
the Angel of the LORD, the eternal Son of God, in the flaming bush
a figure of Israel in affliction, burning yet not consumed because God was in the midst

schools.summary: Gill: Moses said he would turn aside from the place where he was and the flock he was feeding, and get nearer to the bush, which was on one side of him; the witnesses note Moses saying he would turn aside to see the great sight, why the bush was not burnt — the kindling of Moses' wonder and his drawing near to the marvel (the strange sight drawing Moses to look closer: 'I will now turn aside, and see this great sight, why the bush is not burnt'; the wonder awakened, that Moses, seeing the bush blaze yet not burn, was moved with holy curiosity to 'turn aside' from his flock and 'see this great sight'; the question that drew him, 'why the bush is not burnt,' that the marvel was not the fire only but that the fire did not consume, a thing against nature, which made Moses seek to know its cause; that God uses the wonder to draw Moses near, that he might there meet and call him; the readiness of Moses to leave his task and inquire into the things of God when God's hand was so plainly in it).

themes: And Moses said, I will now turn aside, and see this great sight
why the bush is not burnt
Moses moved with holy wonder to draw near the marvel
God using the wonder to draw Moses near, that he might there meet and call him

schools.summary: Gill: he who is before called the Angel of the LORD is here Jehovah, the omniscient and omnipresent Being, who observed Moses turning aside and called to him; the witnesses note the LORD, seeing Moses turn aside, calling to him out of the bush, 'Moses, Moses,' and Moses answering, 'Here am I' — the personal call of God by name, and Moses' ready answer (God, who is the Angel and now named Jehovah, calling his servant: 'God called unto him out of the midst of the bush, and said, Moses, Moses. And he said, Here am I'; the identity confirmed, that 'the LORD' and 'God' here are the same as 'the Angel of the LORD,' so that the Angel of the bush is Jehovah, the omniscient and omnipresent God; the personal and earnest call, 'Moses, Moses,' the doubling of the name marking love, earnestness, and the weight of the call, as God elsewhere calls 'Abraham, Abraham' and 'Samuel, Samuel'; Moses' ready and reverent answer, 'Here am I,' the response of a willing servant, present and attentive to God's voice; that God knows and calls his servants by name, and looks for their ready answer).

themes: God called unto him out of the midst of the bush, and said, Moses, Moses
And he said, Here am I
God's personal call by name, the Angel here named Jehovah
the doubled name marking love and earnestness, Moses' ready 'Here am I'

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: it is likely that from this circumstance all the eastern nations have agreed to perform the acts of religious worship barefooted; the witnesses note God bidding Moses not draw near but put off his shoes, for the place was holy ground — the demand of reverence in the presence of the holy God (God teaching Moses awe before he teaches him his errand: 'Draw not nigh hither: put off thy shoes from off thy feet, for the place whereon thou standest is holy ground'; the charge to keep distance, 'Draw not nigh hither,' that Moses, a sinful man, was not to rush rashly into the immediate presence of the holy God but to keep a reverent distance; the token of reverence required, 'put off thy shoes from off thy feet,' the Eastern sign of respect and submission, putting off the shoes as a lowly servant on entering a holy or honoured place, a custom Clarke notes spread among the eastern nations for worship; the reason, 'for the place whereon thou standest is holy ground,' not that the ground was holy in itself but made holy by the presence of God then manifested there; the great lesson, that God is to be approached with reverence and godly fear, for he is holy).

themes: Draw not nigh hither: put off thy shoes from off thy feet
for the place whereon thou standest is holy ground
God demanding reverence, the ground made holy by his presence
God is to be approached with reverence and godly fear, for he is holy

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: though the word 'father' is here used in the singular, it points to the patriarchs collectively, the God of his fathers; the witnesses note God saying, 'I am the God of thy father, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob,' and Moses hiding his face in fear — the covenant title of God and the awe of Moses before him (God naming himself the covenant God of the patriarchs: 'I am the God of thy father, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob'; the great covenant name, that God makes himself known not by his bare being only but as the God who had covenanted with the fathers, faithful to his promises, now coming to fulfil them in delivering their seed; the weight our Lord himself drew from these words, that 'God is not the God of the dead, but of the living,' proving from this very verse the resurrection and the life of the patriarchs with God; the effect on Moses, 'Moses hid his face; for he was afraid to look upon God,' the awe of a sinful creature before the holy and glorious God, not daring to gaze on his presence; the godly fear that the manifest presence of God works in his servants).

debates.question: What did Christ prove from 'I am the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob'?

sides.position: The resurrection of the dead — for 'God is not the God of the dead, but of the living' (Matthew 22:32); the patriarchs live with God, and the covenant title proves their continued life and coming resurrection

themes: I am the God of thy father, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob
And Moses hid his face; for he was afraid to look upon God
God's covenant title, faithful to fulfil his promises to the fathers
the words from which Christ proved the resurrection, and Moses' holy fear

schools.summary: Gill: 'in seeing I have seen' denotes not only the certainty but the clear, distinct sight he had of their affliction, with sympathy and an affectionate concern, and a fixed determination to deliver them; the witnesses note the LORD saying he had surely seen Israel's affliction, heard their cry, and known their sorrows — the tender compassion of God for his suffering people (God revealing his fatherly heart toward Israel: 'I have surely seen the affliction of my people... and have heard their cry... for I know their sorrows'; the emphasis, 'I have surely seen,' literally 'in seeing I have seen,' which marks not the certainty only but the clear, distinct, and feeling sight God had of their misery, regarding it with sympathy and affectionate concern; the threefold word of compassion, 'seen the affliction... heard their cry... I know their sorrows,' that God beheld their bondage, heard their groaning under their taskmasters, and inwardly knew and felt their grief; the comfort the commentators draw, that God is not indifferent to his people's sufferings but sees, hears, and knows them with a tender and saving regard; the owning of them, 'my people,' the covenant tie that moved his pity).

themes: I have surely seen the affliction of my people which are in Egypt, and have heard their cry
for I know their sorrows
God's tender compassion, seeing, hearing, and knowing his people's affliction
God not indifferent to suffering, but with a tender and saving regard, owning them as his people

schools.summary: Gill: God's coming down is spoken consistent with his omnipresence, who is everywhere and cannot strictly remove from place to place, but such speech is used when he gives some eminent display of his power or goodness, as here in delivering his people; the witnesses note God saying he was come down to deliver Israel from the Egyptians and bring them to a good and large land flowing with milk and honey — the gracious purpose of deliverance and the promise of the good land (God declaring both the rescue and the inheritance: 'I am come down to deliver them out of the hand of the Egyptians... unto a good land and a large, unto a land flowing with milk and honey'; the manner of speech, 'I am come down,' that God, who is everywhere and cannot remove from place to place, is yet said to 'come down' when he gives some great display of his power and goodness, as here to save; the double mercy, 'to deliver them out of the hand of the Egyptians,' the rescue from bondage, and 'to bring them up... unto a good land and a large,' the inheritance promised the fathers; the rich description, 'a land flowing with milk and honey,' a fruitful, abundant land, fitly figuring also the heavenly Canaan and the riches of the gospel; the faithfulness of God, performing to the seed the promise sworn to Abraham).

themes: I am come down to deliver them out of the hand of the Egyptians
unto a good land and a large, unto a land flowing with milk and honey
God's double mercy of deliverance from bondage and the promised inheritance
the abundant land figuring also the heavenly Canaan and the riches of the gospel

schools.summary: Gill: the cry of the children of Israel is come unto him, and he had seen the oppression with which the Egyptians oppressed them; the witnesses note God declaring again that the cry of Israel had come to him and he had seen the oppression of the Egyptians — the renewed assurance that God had heard and seen, the ground of his sending Moses (God stating again the cause that moves him: 'the cry of the children of Israel is come unto me: and I have also seen the oppression wherewith the Egyptians oppress them'; the repetition, pressing the certainty that God had truly heard and seen, that 'the cry of the children of Israel is come unto me,' ascending to heaven and not unheeded; the sight of the wrong, 'I have also seen the oppression wherewith the Egyptians oppress them,' that God beheld the cruel oppression laid on his people, the heavy bondage and bitter usage; that this declaration is the ground laid for the commission that follows, God's compassion moving him to send a deliverer; the comfort, that the cries of the oppressed do come up to God, who will avenge his elect).

themes: the cry of the children of Israel is come unto me
and I have also seen the oppression wherewith the Egyptians oppress them
God's renewed assurance that he heard the cry and saw the oppression
the cries of the oppressed come up to God, who will avenge his elect

schools.summary: John Calvin: after God had furnished his servant with promises to engage him more cheerfully in his work, he now adds commands and calls him to undertake the office to which he is designed, and this is the best encouragement to duty, when God renders those who would otherwise be slow more cheerful by his promises; the witnesses note God bidding Moses come and be sent to Pharaoh, to bring forth his people Israel out of Egypt — the great commission of Moses, joined to God's gracious promises (God, having shown his compassion, now laying the charge: 'Come now therefore, and I will send thee unto Pharaoh, that thou mayest bring forth my people the children of Israel out of Egypt'; the order Calvin marks, that God first 'furnished his servant with promises to engage him more cheerfully' and then 'adds commands,' so that the promise goes before the precept, the best encouragement to duty; the charge, 'I will send thee unto Pharaoh,' that Moses must leave his flock and family and go to the mighty king to demand Israel's release; the end of the sending, 'that thou mayest bring forth my people the children of Israel out of Egypt,' that Moses should be God's instrument to lead out the covenant people; the honour and weight of so great a commission, God working his deliverance by a man).

themes: Come now therefore, and I will send thee unto Pharaoh
that thou mayest bring forth my people the children of Israel out of Egypt
God's great commission of Moses, the promise going before the precept
God working his deliverance by a man, Moses his instrument to lead out his people

schools.summary: Gill: Moses pleaded he was a private person, an exile, a poor shepherd, unknown to Pharaoh, while Pharaoh was a great king with numerous forces; the witnesses note Moses asking, 'Who am I, that I should go unto Pharaoh, and that I should bring forth the children of Israel?' — the diffidence of Moses, a sense of his own weakness and unfitness for the task (Moses shrinking back, conscious of his lowliness: 'Who am I, that I should go unto Pharaoh, and that I should bring forth the children of Israel out of Egypt?'; the ground of his hesitation, that Moses felt himself 'a private person, an exile in a foreign country, a poor shepherd,' unknown to and without interest with Pharaoh, 'a great king' with mighty forces, and so unequal to so great a task; the mingled humility and weakness in this, that Moses, who forty years before had been forward, is now so abased that he shrinks from the work; that such a sense of one's own insufficiency is fitting in itself, yet must not be suffered to refuse God's call, for the sufficiency is of God, not of the man; that God meets the objection not by exalting Moses but by promising his own presence).

themes: Who am I, that I should go unto Pharaoh
and that I should bring forth the children of Israel out of Egypt?
Moses' diffidence, a sense of his own weakness and unfitness
a humility fitting in itself, yet not to refuse God's call, for the sufficiency is of God

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: God said, Certainly I will be with thee, and gave him a sign that he had sent him, that when he had brought forth the people they should serve God upon that mountain; Gill: God would be with Moses to encourage, strengthen, protect, defend, and prosper him; the witnesses note God promising, 'Certainly I will be with thee,' and giving a token, that they should serve God on that mountain — the all-sufficient answer to Moses' weakness, the presence of God and the pledge of success (God meeting the 'Who am I?' not with Moses' worth but with his own presence: 'Certainly I will be with thee; and this shall be a token unto thee... ye shall serve God upon this mountain'; the heart of the answer, 'Certainly I will be with thee,' that it matters not who Moses is, for God will be with him to encourage, strengthen, protect, defend, and prosper him, the presence of God being the strength of the weak servant; the token given, 'this shall be a token unto thee, that I have sent thee: When thou hast brought forth the people out of Egypt, ye shall serve God upon this mountain,' a sign to confirm his faith, that the very place of his calling should be the place where the redeemed people would worship God, the deliverance assured beforehand; that the token was a thing of faith, to be believed before it was seen, resting on God's word; that God's presence and promise are enough to silence every plea of weakness).

debates.question: How does God answer Moses' 'Who am I?'

sides.position: Not by exalting Moses or pointing to his worth, but by promising his own presence — 'Certainly I will be with thee' — and giving a token of faith; the presence of God is the strength of the weak servant, enough to silence every plea of weakness

themes: Certainly I will be with thee
ye shall serve God upon this mountain
God answering Moses' weakness with the promise of his own presence
a token of faith, the presence of God the strength of the weak servant

schools.summary: Gill: Moses, having received satisfaction to his first objection, raises another difficulty, that the people might ask by what name God had revealed himself; the witnesses note Moses asking God what he should answer if Israel asked the name of the God who had sent him — the desire of Moses to have a clear warrant, and the occasion of the great revelation of the name (Moses seeking the name to give: 'when I come unto the children of Israel... and they shall say to me, What is his name? what shall I say unto them?'; the prudence of Moses, wishing his commission to be plain and firm, that he might tell the people exactly who had sent him; the likely reason of the question, that the people, in their long bondage, might ask by what particular name God now made himself known, or seek some fresh token of the God of their fathers; that this honest inquiry became the occasion of God's revealing his great and proper name; that it is right to seek a clear warrant for God's work, that we may speak with authority).

themes: when I come unto the children of Israel... The God of your fathers hath sent me unto you
and they shall say to me, What is his name? what shall I say unto them?
Moses seeking a clear warrant, what name to give Israel
an honest inquiry that occasioned God's revealing of his great and proper name

schools.summary: Gill: 'I AM THAT I AM' signifies the real being of God, his self-existence, that he is the Being of beings, his eternity and immutability, and his constancy and faithfulness in fulfilling his promises, for it includes all time past, present, and to come; the witnesses note God answering Moses, 'I AM THAT I AM,' and bidding him say, 'I AM hath sent me unto you' — the supreme revelation of God's name, declaring his self-existence, eternity, and unchanging faithfulness (God revealing himself by the name above all names: 'I AM THAT I AM... Thus shalt thou say unto the children of Israel, I AM hath sent me unto you'; the depth of the name, that 'I AM THAT I AM' declares 'the real being of God, his self-existence,' that he alone truly is, 'the Being of beings,' depending on none, the source of all; that it denotes his 'eternity and immutability,' for the name includes all time, past, present, and to come, he who was, and is, and is to come, ever the same; the comfort drawn for Israel, that this unchanging, ever-living God would surely keep his promises and deliver them, his constancy and faithfulness in fulfilling his promises; the great fulfilment the commentators mark, that the Lord Jesus took this very name to himself, 'Before Abraham was, I AM,' declaring his eternal Godhead).

debates.question: What does the name 'I AM THAT I AM' reveal of God?

sides.position: His self-existence (the Being of beings, depending on none), his eternity and immutability (including all time past, present, and to come), and his constancy and faithfulness to fulfil his promises — the ground of Israel's hope of deliverance

debates.question: How is the name 'I AM' connected to Christ?

sides.position: The Lord Jesus took this very name to himself — 'Before Abraham was, I AM' (John 8:58) — declaring his eternal Godhead and identity with the self-existent God of the bush

themes: I AM THAT I AM
I AM hath sent me unto you
the supreme name declaring God's self-existence, eternity, and faithfulness
the name Christ took to himself, 'Before Abraham was, I AM'

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the name here referred to is Jehovah Elohim, the Lord God, the name by which God is known as the fountain of being and the keeper of covenant; the witnesses note God bidding Moses say the LORD God of the fathers, of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, had sent him, this being his name for ever and his memorial to all generations — the joining of the eternal name to the covenant name, God's everlasting memorial (God knitting 'I AM' to 'the God of the fathers': 'The LORD God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, hath sent me unto you: this is my name for ever'; the two names joined, the proper name 'the LORD,' Jehovah, the self-existent I AM, and the covenant name 'the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob,' so that the eternal God is also the faithful covenant-keeper of the fathers; the permanence, 'this is my name for ever, and this is my memorial unto all generations,' that this name, Jehovah, should be the abiding name and memorial by which God would be known and remembered through all ages; the comfort, that the unchanging I AM is the same God who covenanted with the fathers and would keep covenant with their seed; that the name is God's chosen memorial, by which his people should call upon and remember him for ever).

themes: The LORD God of your fathers... hath sent me unto you
this is my name for ever, and this is my memorial unto all generations
God joining the eternal name to the covenant name of the fathers
the unchanging I AM, the same God who covenanted with the fathers and keeps covenant with their seed

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: Moses is here instructed what to say to the elders of Israel, the heads of the tribes or families; Gill: not all the ancient men, nor judges, but the heads of tribes or families; the witnesses note God bidding Moses gather the elders of Israel and tell them the LORD had appeared to him, saying he had surely visited them and seen their affliction — the charge to carry the good news of God's visitation to the elders (Moses sent first to the leaders of the people: 'gather the elders of Israel together, and say unto them, The LORD God of your fathers... appeared unto me, saying, I have surely visited you'; the means appointed, 'gather the elders of Israel together,' the heads of the tribes and families, that through them the word might come to all the people, an orderly way; the message of comfort, 'The LORD God of your fathers... appeared unto me,' that the covenant God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob had now revealed himself and was about to act; the gracious visitation, 'I have surely visited you, and seen that which is done to you in Egypt,' that God had come to look upon and to help them, surely visited them in mercy, having seen their oppression; that this visitation was the dawn of their deliverance, the long-awaited remembrance of God's promise).

themes: Go, and gather the elders of Israel together
I have surely visited you, and seen that which is done to you in Egypt
Moses sent to bring the elders the good news of God's gracious visitation
the dawn of deliverance, the long-awaited remembrance of God's promise

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: God said, I will bring you up out of the affliction of Egypt unto the land of the Canaanites and the other nations, a land flowing with milk and honey; Gill: God had resolved in his own mind and declared it; the witnesses note God declaring his settled resolve to bring Israel out of affliction to the land flowing with milk and honey — the renewed promise of deliverance and inheritance, God's fixed purpose (God sealing to the elders the promise of the good land: 'I have said, I will bring you up out of the affliction of Egypt... unto a land flowing with milk and honey'; the settledness, 'I have said,' that God had resolved in his own mind and declared it, his fixed and unchangeable purpose to save them; the double mercy again, 'bring you up out of the affliction of Egypt,' the deliverance from bondage, and 'unto a land flowing with milk and honey,' the good inheritance of Canaan, then held by the Canaanites and the other nations named; the certainty of the promise, resting on the word of the unchanging I AM, who cannot fail to perform; the comfort to the afflicted people, that their misery should end in a land of plenty and rest; the type the commentators draw, of the believer's deliverance from sin's bondage to the heavenly inheritance).

themes: I will bring you up out of the affliction of Egypt
unto a land flowing with milk and honey
God's settled resolve, his fixed and unchangeable purpose to save
the double mercy of deliverance and inheritance, resting on the word of the unchanging I AM

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: they shall hearken to thy voice, a promise that the elders would give credit to his commission; the witnesses note God promising that the elders would hearken, and bidding Moses with them ask Pharaoh leave for a three days' journey to sacrifice — the assurance of the elders' faith and the first modest request to Pharaoh (God foretelling success with the elders and directing the suit to the king: 'they shall hearken to thy voice... ye shall say unto him, The LORD God of the Hebrews hath met with us: and now let us go... three days' journey into the wilderness, that we may sacrifice'; the encouragement, 'they shall hearken to thy voice,' that, unlike his first rejection, the elders would now give credit to his commission, attend, and obey, God going before to open their hearts; the manner of the request to Pharaoh, 'The LORD God of the Hebrews hath met with us: and now let us go... three days' journey... that we may sacrifice to the LORD our God,' a modest and reasonable suit, asking leave to go and worship, that Pharaoh's refusal might be without excuse; the wisdom of the demand, framed to lay bare Pharaoh's hard heart, since even so small a request he would deny; that God orders the means as well as the end).

themes: they shall hearken to thy voice
let us go, we beseech thee, three days' journey into the wilderness, that we may sacrifice to the LORD our God
God promising the elders would hearken, and directing a modest suit to Pharaoh
a reasonable request framed to lay bare Pharaoh's hard heart

schools.summary: Gill: though so reasonable a request was made, it would not be granted; this is observed that they might not be discouraged when he should refuse to dismiss them, which the omniscient God knew; the witnesses note God declaring he was sure the king of Egypt would not let them go, not even by a mighty hand — the foreknowledge of Pharaoh's hard refusal, told beforehand to forewarn the people (God revealing the resistance to come: 'I am sure that the king of Egypt will not let you go, no, not by a mighty hand'; God's certain foreknowledge, 'I am sure,' that the omniscient God knew Pharaoh's heart and foretold that the king 'will not let you go,' for all the reasonableness of the request; the force of 'no, not by a mighty hand,' variously taken, that Pharaoh would not yield even when a mighty hand, God's in the plagues, was stretched against him until it was fully shown, or that he would not let them go except by a mighty hand compelling him; the purpose of the warning, that the people 'might not be discouraged' when Pharaoh refused, knowing it was foretold and part of God's plan; that God's foreknowledge of opposition does not hinder but forwards his purpose, his power being made known in the overcoming).

themes: And I am sure that the king of Egypt will not let you go
no, not by a mighty hand
God's foreknowledge of Pharaoh's hard refusal, told beforehand
the warning given that the people might not be discouraged when he refused

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: hands are ascribed to God after the manner of men, denoting his power put forth in action; the witnesses note God promising to stretch out his hand and smite Egypt with all his wonders, after which Pharaoh would let them go — the assurance that God's mighty wonders would break Pharaoh's resistance (God answering Pharaoh's hardness with his almighty power: 'I will stretch out my hand, and smite Egypt with all my wonders... and after that he will let you go'; the figure, 'I will stretch out my hand,' God's power put forth in action, spoken after the manner of men, the hand of the Almighty raised against Egypt; the means, 'smite Egypt with all my wonders which I will do in the midst thereof,' the plagues, the great wonders God would work in Egypt to humble Pharaoh and judge his gods; the certain issue, 'and after that he will let you go,' that all Pharaoh's resistance should at last be broken, and he would be forced to let Israel go, God's power prevailing over the proudest enemy; that what man's reason could not win, God's mighty hand would surely accomplish).

themes: I will stretch out my hand, and smite Egypt with all my wonders which I will do in the midst thereof
and after that he will let you go
God's almighty wonders, the plagues, breaking Pharaoh's resistance
what man's reason could not win, God's mighty hand would accomplish

schools.summary: Gill: God would give the Israelites favour in the sight of the Egyptians, who would be ready to do or bestow anything for them, or lend what they desired; the witnesses note God promising to give his people favour with the Egyptians, so that they should not go out empty — the gracious promise that the delivered people should depart enriched, not destitute (God providing that Israel leave Egypt with their wages: 'I will give this people favour in the sight of the Egyptians: and... when ye go, ye shall not go empty'; the turning of hearts, 'I will give this people favour in the sight of the Egyptians,' that God, who holds all hearts, would so incline the Egyptians, humbled by the plagues, that they should be willing, even eager, to give to the Israelites; the promise, 'ye shall not go empty,' that the people, after long years of unrequited toil, should not depart bare and destitute but laden with goods, a kind of recompense for their hard bondage; the justice and mercy in this, that God would see his people righted and provided for, sending them out with substance; that God can turn the hearts of enemies to favour his own when he pleases).

themes: I will give this people favour in the sight of the Egyptians
that, when ye go, ye shall not go empty
God promising favour, that Israel should depart enriched, not destitute
a recompense for their long unrequited toil; God turns the hearts of enemies to favour his own

schools.summary: Gill: the word rendered 'borrow' rather means to 'ask' or desire them to give or lend; by this it appears the Israelites were spread about and mixed with the Egyptians; the witnesses note God bidding every woman ask of her neighbour jewels of silver and gold and raiment, to put on their children, and so spoil the Egyptians — the manner of the enriching, the asking of goods that justly recompensed and spoiled Egypt (God showing how Israel should be enriched at their departure: 'every woman shall borrow of her neighbour... jewels of silver, and jewels of gold, and raiment... and ye shall spoil the Egyptians'; the true sense of 'borrow,' that the word rather means to 'ask' or 'desire,' not a fraudulent borrowing with intent never to return but an asking of gifts which the Egyptians, glad to be rid of them, freely gave; the things asked, 'jewels of silver, and jewels of gold, and raiment,' to be put 'upon your sons, and upon your daughters,' that the whole family should be enriched with the goods of Egypt; the justice the commentators mark, 'ye shall spoil the Egyptians,' that this was a righteous recompense for the long, unpaid labour of Israel, the spoiling of those who had spoiled them, and so no wrong but justice; God's right to dispose of all men's goods as he will, righting his oppressed people).

debates.question: Was Israel's 'borrowing' and 'spoiling' of the Egyptians a fraud?

sides.position: No — the word rendered 'borrow' rather means to 'ask' or 'desire'; the Egyptians, humbled and glad to be rid of them, freely gave; and the spoiling was a righteous recompense for Israel's long unpaid labour, God's right to dispose of all goods to right his oppressed people

themes: every woman shall borrow of her neighbour... jewels of silver, and jewels of gold, and raiment
and ye shall spoil the Egyptians
God directing the asking of gifts that enriched Israel and spoiled Egypt
not a fraudulent borrowing but a righteous recompense for Israel's unpaid labour
